Title: The essence and transformation of Agent: Agent = LLM + Tools
Text:
The essence and transformation of Agent: Agent = LLM + Tools
In the last article, we talked about the relationship between Tool, MCP and Agent. Simply put, Agent = LLM + Tools, while MCP unifies the process of developing and using Tools.
The article was very popular, and many of my friends had a discussion with me. However, I found that some of my friends still have doubts and misunderstandings about Agent:
- some people assert that “Model is Agent”, and the Agent is just a transition and will eventually be replaced by a more powerful base model.
- other think that generic Agent is more advanced than verticalAgent, and believe that “generic Agent” will be realized soon.
- more commonly, all kinds of products that automate tasks are loosely referred to as Agent.
This cognitive confusion stems from the fact that Anthropic, Google, OpenAI and other leading companies have not yet reached a consensus on the precise definition of Agent, and are still “talking their own language”; on the other hand, the media’s over-hype and selective interpretation have also caused the word Agent to be seriously diluted and generalized, almost losing its technical connotation.
So, what exactly is an agent? and how is it related to the LLM, Tools, and Workflow? What profound changes will it bring to the development of AI?
Today, I will combine 302.AI’s frontline practical experience to help you clarify the essence of Agent and share my judgment on the future development pattern.
1. Agent = LLM + Tools
Finding Consensus in the Hustle
2025, the year of the recognized Agent. What followed was the proliferation of the word. Articles, podcasts, interviews, presentations, closed meetings, white papers, …… Everyone is talking about Agents, but the more they listen, the more confused they become.
TechCrunch, a well-known tech media, even wrote an article complaining that “No one knows what the hell an AI agent is”.
Simon Willison, a well-known tech blogger, once launched a challenge at X : give Agent a generic definition in 280 words.
this month, Latent Space owner Swyx revisited the matter at the 2025 AI Engineer Summit, showing off a variety of answers in the comments section (pictured above) — a full-screen definition that epitomizes the current cognitive confusion.
Months have passed,and even industry giants have not been able to fully unify their statements. The chaos continues. Let’s take a look at a few representative views:
Anthropic《Building effective agents》
At Anthropic, we categorize all these variations as agentic systems, but draw an important architectural distinction between workflows and agents；
Workflows are systems where LLMs and tools are orchestrated through predefined code paths.
Agents, on the other hand, are systems where LLMs dynamically direct their own processes and tool usage, maintaining control over how they accomplish tasks.（2024.10）OpenAI 《A practical guide to building agents》
Agents are systems that independently accomplish tasks on your behalf. （2025.4）Google《Agents》
A Generative AI agent can be defined as an application that attempts to achieve a goal by observing the world and acting upon it using the tools that it has at its disposal. （2024.12）Lilian Weng《LLM Powered Autonomous Agents》
In a LLM-powered autonomous agent system, LLM functions as the agent’s brain, complemented by several key components: Planning, Memory, Tool use. (2023.6)
Each has a long document explaining what the Agent is. But few people will really read it carefully.
Actually, it’s not that complicated, we can grab the most concise and core formula: Agent = LLM + Tools
This is enough to reveal the core mechanism of the AI Agent:LLM responsible for thinking and calling, Tools responsible for executing and returning results. With this, you have the key to the world of Agents.
2. LLM
Models have no memory.
In order to better understand the following paragraphs, we also need to mention one of the basic features of large language models (LLM).
Why can’t a model replace an agent ? Because the model (LLM) itself has no memory, the technical term is stateless.
This is an extremely important but easily overlooked fact. Generally speaking, no matter how many rounds of LLM conversation you’ve had with a person before, it doesn’t “remember” any historical information on its own. With each interaction, it’s just processing what you’re currently typing. The state of the model itself does not change due to past inputs and outputs.
So why do we often feel like AI “remembers” what we’ve said and is able to have coherent conversations over multiple rounds?
It’s actually a kind of simulated memory. Every time the application (such as ChatGPT) sends a new request to the model, it will actively package the previous conversation history and send it to the model as a context. The model responds on the basis of this temporary, externally provided “memory”, thus creating the “illusion” of a coherent dialogue.
This kind of externally stored “memory” can be stored locally on your device or on a cloud server, in short, it is not inside the model. This design decouples model computation from session state management, so that the same model can serve a large number of different users and sessions at the same time without interfering with each other.
We can imagine the model (LLM) as a computer in an Internet cafe: it restarts after each use (after a dialog request is processed, the LLM internally restores its initial state), and so on. The pre-training data is like its pre-installed operating system.
Note: It is another thing to incorporate memory data into model weights through fine-tuning to form a private model, which will not be discussed for the time being.
3. Tools
Evolution from Q&A to Loop
It is because LLM is stateless and good at handling single inputs and outputs, which makes it ideal for Q&A, that its original and most natural application form is the Chatbot.
The chatbot interaction mode is basically a cycle of“human-AI-human-AI — ….” In this process, humans are constantly guiding the AI by asking questions, pursuing and clarifying to make its output gradually meet expectations. This stage is called Human in the Loop.
Whether it’s a single round of Q&A, or multiple rounds of conversations that are passed into the historical context through the program, they are not inherently out of this mode.
With the improvement of LLM capabilities, people gradually find that AI answers are becoming more and more reliable, and many links no longer require human intervention. And so a revolutionary idea was born: Can AI “talk” to itself? In other words, can AI be self-driven as it performs tasks?
We call this new logic of “Human — AI — AI — AI — ….”as Human on the loop. Humans only need to set an initial goal, and all subsequent steps are completed by AI autonomously.
This is one of the most important things about Agents: self-circulation.
By realizing this “self-loop”, LLM has the ability to call tools. For example, the Function Call, which we are most familiar with, is a typical LLM self-looping process
Human Ask a question → AI proposes to call a tool to assist in answering → tool automatically executes and returns the result → AI receives the result and determines whether to continue the loop or not.
A common misconception to point out here is that most app clients present the above processes in the same conversational interface, making users mistakenly believe that there is only one AI Q&A. But in reality, the AI needs to answer twice for every call to the tool.
This is also the fundamental reason why it LLM cannot replace the Agent :LLM it is not possible to call a tool in a single answer, and get the result of the tool call.
4. Agent Framework
Three types of loops
The model learns to loop itself, and then the AI suddenly “realizes” that I don’t have to answer directly this time! I can request a tool to call, get information from outside the model, and give it to myself in the next loop. Then in the next loop, answer based on this new information!
So who controls the looping process?LLM itself can’t do it, so it’s necessary to rely on external code built by humans to receive requests and pass on information. This kind of code is called the Agent framework, which essentially controls the AI to loop itself and maintain its memory.
I’ve divided the current Agent framework into three categories:
1: Manual Agent Framework
Developers pre-set a plan for each step of task execution, clearly define which step to use which tool, and LLM is mainly responsible for populating content or making simple decisions at the pre-set nodes.
I call it Workflow, and it’s a white-box system.
Dify and Coze are typical examples of tools that provide visual process orchestration. At this point the execution steps of the Tools are in many cases enforced by humans in exchange for more certainty.
2: Semi-automatic agent frameworks
The AI is preset as a vertical agent with different identities (system prompts+ specific tools), each vertical agent completes different subtasks, and finally combines the execution process and results of each subtask through the framework to complete the final goal.
I call this the Multi-Agent System, and it’s a gray-box system.
The planning pattern of Manus and coze space are typical multi-agent Frame. Planning and memory management are both part of the orchestration.
3: Full Automated Agent framework
Set only one end goal for the model, and the model will begin to loop itself until it achieves the goal or encounters an unsolvable obstacle.
The fully automated agent framework is the most concise, and the few lines of code that call the tool are the core of all of them, and all the complex processes are left to the model to solve.
I call this the Single-Agent System, also known as the General Agent, and it’s a black-box system.
The model completes the operation of the tool call independently, which is a fully automatic agent framework, if the model doesn’t have a Tool Use functionality, which can also be implemented through code
It is important to emphasize that these three frameworks are not opposites, but are often used in combination. For example, a Multi-Agent system can be combined with Workflow, and a Single-Agent system is an important part of both Workflow and Multi-Agent systems.
Unlock new achievements ✦✦
Reading here, you can smoothly access the Agent white paper released by Anthropic , Google, and OpenAI (see the end of the article for the link, the same below)!
And, you start to be comfortable in this field. Even when you saw the Langchain team post a long article complaining that OpenAI’s concept was unclear, would cast a look of approval.
5. Judgment first: There is a limit to a single-agent framework
Multi-agent systems is still the mainstream
With the release of new models such as OpenAI o3 and o4-mini, we have observed a significant improvement in the ability of model agents to be more proficient in using more tools and performing longer step chains to complete complex tasks. The essence of these advances is that the number of times the model loops itself has increased.
Just like a spinning top, it may fall crooked after two turns in the past, and it needs to be constantly “whipped”; Now it has a more powerful motor built-in, which is able to rotate stably for longer periods of time.
At this point, a voice comes in: the model is replacing the Agent, or the Model is Agent. In fact, the more rigorous and accurate technical expression of this sentence is: the Single-Agent system will replace the Workflow and Multi-Agent systems; In other words, the general agent will rule the world.
But I think this can only be a beautiful vision, and it is impossible to achieve it in the short term.
Reason 1: Controllability
The output of LLM is inherently random. For a complex task that requires multiple steps to complete, if you rely solely on the capabilities of the model itself, then small deviations in each step of the decision can be accumulated and magnified, ultimately leading to a false result.
Again, use the spinning top as an example. No matter how strong the initial power of a spinning top is, no one can guarantee that it will eventually stop at the desired position when it is unsupervised and uncontrolled.
Reason 2: Lack of model capabilities
At present, the mainstream LLM training and assessment revolve around single-round (or limited-round) question-and-answer tasks, and there is a lack of training and evaluation for “long-chain real-world tasks”. This leads to an inherent deficit LLM in the ability to accomplish complex tasks.
We are desperately lacking LLM the high-quality action sequence data required for agent training. For example, when training a Search task, it’s important to evaluate the model’s ability to get information, generate intermediate steps, adjust plans based on feedback, and even backtrack and retry. I even suspect that even big companies like OpenAI or Anthropic don’t have access to enough of this data.
Therefore, the only way to AGI is LLM Become an agent and do continuous reinforcement learning in the real world, and eventually it is possible to surpass humans. LLM Training is also moving towards a new era in which agents interact directly and continuously with the environment to gain first-hand experience (what Sutton calls the Era of Experience).
The LangChain team once conducted a survey on the agent and found that the biggest bottleneck affecting its implementation is “performance quality”, and the source of the performance quality bottleneck is the large model.
Here is a clear conclusion: limited by LLM the current controllability and ability, expect an all-round model + concise single Agent system packages are unrealistic in the short term. Multi-Agent systems will be the dominant paradigm for building complex, reliable Agent applications for a long time to come.
Unlock new achievements ✦✦
By reading this, you’ll have silky access to almost all of the popular must-read articles!
For example, Shunyu Yao’s 《The Second Half》，Richard Sutton’s latest essay 《Welcome to the Era of Experience》 and the famous 《The Bitter Lesson》
6. Judgment second: From Q&A to task delegation
AI usage has changed dramatically
With the advent of the Agent era, one obvious change is ：our interaction with AI is shifting from “answering questions instantly” to “completing tasks asynchronously.”
In the past, we asked AI a question and expected it to answer it right away. Now and in the future, we give the Agent a complex task that can take minutes or even hours to work independently and ultimately deliver a complete result. This transformation, seemingly simple and natural, will have a series of far-reaching implications.
Impact 1: Token consumption skyrockets
In the past, the rate of token consumption was limited by the input and reading speed of humans (about 4 tokens per second).
Now that AI is self-circulating, the token consumption rate depends on the model’s reasoning and generation speed (up to hundreds of tokens per second or more), and the machine can work 24/7, so the token consumption will increase exponentially. This will also pose unprecedented challenges to the concurrency and reasoning speed of model services.
Impact 2: The form of the AI product changes
When the core of interaction shifts from “process” to “outcome”, when users no longer need to wait for AI’s response in real time, and when users change from “AI creators” to “AI consumers”, how will the product form develop?
Impact 3: Vertical Agent Ecosystem Explodes
As mentioned above, complex tasks require multi-agent collaboration. This means that the market needs a large number of [vertical agents] that focus on solving specific areas and specific types of problems.
The development of vertical agents and the interconnection between them (such as ANP and A2A, which we discussed in the previous article), will usher in the next big wave of entrepreneurship and innovation.
Unlock new achievements ✦✦
Reading this, you can understand that Baidu Cloud, Alibaba Cloud, Tencent Cloud, and Volcano Cloud have been making frequent moves recently, and the significance of accessing MCP and then betting on the Agent ecosystem lies: they all want to be their own verticals Agent Development platform.
7. Judgment third: Closed source VS Open source
Supporting a huge Agent eco-system together
The explosion of the agent ecosystem has shifted the user’s focus from “which model to use” to “which agent can best complete my task”. This will not only reshape the form of the product, but also profoundly change the business model of AI.
I believe that in the future, the Agent ecosystem may have a commercial development path that is parallel to closed source and open source.
Path 1: Closed source agent ecosystem
Some model vendors will no longer provide model interfaces, but directly provide agent interfaces. Alexander Doria and Naveen Rao (VP of Databricks) expressed the same opinion some time ago and gave a clear timeframe: it will only take 2–3 years, or even faster.
GPT-5, which has been rumored for a long time, may appear in this form, LLM directly encapsulating it with tools.
In the case that the general ability of the basic model is limited, the training of specific tools can be used to improve the effect of the final completion of the task. In doing so, the model capability can be continuously strengthened through the feedback of the user’s real-world tasks, forming a data flywheel.
Unlock new achievements ✦✦
If you observe closely, it’s easy to notice that Agent tools like OpenAI Deep Research and Anthropic Claude Code don’t provide APIs. The consciousness of the top model manufacturers is consistent and first-rate.
Path 2: Open Source Agent Ecosystem
Another part of the model providers (especially open source model providers, such as DeepSeek, etc.) will work with the vast developer community to build an open and diverse agent ecosystem.
The threshold for agent development will be much lower than that of traditional software, which allows more ordinary people to participate in it, and integrate their own unique experience to develop a variety of agents to meet massive long-tail needs.
On the one hand, the closed-source giants use the data flywheel to form a large-scale effect, and on the other hand, the people contribute their unique experience to the open source community. This is bound to be an unprecedentedly huge market.
Unlock new achievements ✦✦
Reading this, you’ll have a silky tap into Alexander Doria’s long article “The Model is the Product”, which exploded last month! I don’t completely agree with the title of the article, but I recognize the path of thinking and judgment behind it.
8. Summary
Let’s go back to the core of this article again: Agent= LLM +Tools
- AI gains the ability to interact with the real world by invoking tools, and then loops itself through interactive feedback to complete tasks. That’s the essence of the Agent.
- The code that controls the self-looping of AI is the Agent framework.
- The multi-agent system will be the mainstream architecture at this stage due to its controllability and reliability advantages in complex tasks.
- In the Agent era, the use of AI will increase exponentially, the form of AI products will change, and the wave of vertical Agent ecology will also be opened.
- Agents will emerge in both closed-source and open-source ecosystems, which together will give rise to an unprecedentedly large market.
Agent is a new type of Scaling Law.
In the history of human development for thousands of years, the growth of our individual intelligence has long been flattened, but the overall scientific and technological level of human civilization is accelerating. The key driver behind this is that humans have learned to use tools and to collaborate at scale.
It is very likely that the silicon-based intelligence we have created is undergoing exactly the same evolutionary process.
Unlock new achievements ✦✦
Hopefully, this article, will help you better understand the profound changes that are happening in the Agent space and take your place faster in the upcoming new era.

Title: Cannot Measure Productivity

Comments.Author: dirtyaura
Comments.Text: The quest for a single measure of hard-to-define concept like productivity is doomed. Even Fowler's article highlights the fact that we don't have a shared understanding what the word productivity means: writong quality code, shipping useful products or making money? all of them? It's no surprise that there is no numerical measurement that captures a badly-defined concept.In my opinion, we should approach measurement from a different angle: can we learn something useful about our profession by combining different types of measurements. Can we, for example, easily spot a person who is doing what Fowler is calling important supportive work. Can we detect problem categories that easily lead to buggy code and allocate more time for code quality work for tasks and less for those that are known to be more straight-forward.
Comments.label: irrelevant

Comments.Author: RogerL
Comments.Text: There will probably be a decent algorithm to do it in the future How could that be possible? Consider the following, very typical scenerio.You write 2000 lines of code, implementing a feature. I write 2,200 lines of code, implementing a feature in a way that supports our vertical for the next 5 years (you just take my module and plug it in, instead of coding from scratch). Add in whatever time interval you want to make it more complicated - I took the same amount of time as you, less, or more.Consider that this is a judgement call - did I or you do the right thing? We understand the risks and costs of premature design, but also understand the risk of coding exactly to today's requirements, with no insight into the future. No algorithm is going to tell you the right answer, and by the time we know (5 years from now) the measurement will be useless.Or we each write a heuristic to the TSP. What algorithm could possible decide whose work is more "productive"? I put it in scare quotes because I don't even know how to define productivity in that regard. Yours runs faster, mine took 1/2 the time to code. Yours is 70% larger than mine, which has cache coherence implications as we continue to add to our programs. Yours is well documented (give me an algorithm to tell if code is 'well' documented), mine is sparse. I used gotos, you used exceptions to deal with errors. You wrote it in Haskell, I did it in C++. Yours will make features X,Y,and Z easily possible, mine makes A,B, and C easy. Your heuristic performs better for some graphs, mine performs better on others.Who is more 'productive' here? It's not even a meaningful question. Bottom line is, we both tackled a hard problem, both did fine in very different ways, and both have implications on the future of the company (assume a,b,c,x,y,z are really important in the future).It's an N-dimensional optimization problem with endless unknowns, and no knowledge or agreement on how to measure many of the axis', let alone their relative importance to each other.
Comments.label: irrelevant

Author: Rafael Goulart
Title: My Week With GitHub Copilot: AI Pair Programming Review
Text:
My Week With GitHub Copilot: AI Pair Programming Review
Before text and code editors, developers were tasked with having the correct code passed to a compiler. Now, support tools like syntax highlighting, function syntax assistance, autolinting, and Artificial Intelligence (AI) assistance are available directly in your Integrated Development Environment (IDE) or the editor where you code.
With these tools, developers can focus on building great software and spend less time completing tedious tasks or combing through code to discover minor errors. For example, ubiquitous code highlighting reminds developers to finish their strings. There’s no need to keep a programming language reference open in a browser tab now that developers can start the function name and the syntax parameter will pop up. If developers forget to close a bracket, the code formatting tool Prettier triggers a reminder to enforce a consistent code style. For those who use tabs or spaces for indentation, now whatever is set up on a project is automatically applied when the file is saved. Overall, these tools help developers reduce errors and improve the quality of their code.
Now, there’s a tool that promises to improve the developer experience even more: GithHub Copilot, an AI pair programmer. After a year of closed beta, GitHub Copilot is now publicly available to developers and is gaining a lot of attention for its ability to enable developers to harness AI while writing code. According to GitHub, developers can write a comment describing the logic they want and GitHub Copilot will automatically generate a code suggestion for their solution. It sounds pretty cool, right? Our team thought so, but we had some questions, for instance:
- Does GitHub Copilot really speed up daily work?
- Are the suggestions valid, or just noise for the developer?
- Is it worth a freelance developer paying for it?
- Is it worth companies paying for it for their team members?
- Does it make you less of a developer if you use a tool like this?
OK, that last one might be a bit philosophical, but the others felt testable. So, to get some answers, I put GitHub Copilot to the test. In this article, I’ll share my experience after one week using the tool and dive into key findings from this trial period. I’ll also use my 20+ years of experience as a Senior Full-Stack Developer to compare it with my day-to-day before Copilot. Hopefully, my experience will give other developers a better understanding of how this tool works and if it lives up to the hype.
Table Of Contents
- What Is GitHub Copilot?
- Testing GitHub Copilot with JavaScript and PHP in Visual Studio
- GitHub Copilot Installation in Visual Studio Code
- GitHub Copilot Shortcuts
- Initial Tests
- Real-Life Action
- AI GitHub Copilot vs Search Engine Results
- Main Concerns About GitHub Copilot
- Alternatives to GitHub Copilot
- Key Takeaways from My Week with GitHub Copilot
- Final conclusions
What Is GitHub Copilot?
To understand how Copilot came to be, it’s first important to understand pair programming.
Pair programming is an extreme programming technique where two developers work together on one computer, one acting as the pilot and the other the copilot. The pilot is on the keyboard while the copilot helps navigate, checking the logic and patterns, while weighing against alternative solutions. While two developers producing one piece of code sounds expensive, the quality of the final work can help avoid added costs of code review and QA.
It was from this idea that AI pair programming came to life. Officially launched in June 2022, GitHub Copilot is an AI pair programming editor that suggests code in real time to help developers work faster and more efficiently. It’s powered by a new AI system, OpenAI Codex, and translates natural language to coding prompts as developers write a comment or a line of code. While it’s free for students, it’s $10/month or $100/year for developers (with a 60-day trial).

Comments.Author: aworks
Comments.Text: "If you treat a prompt as an algorithm, this tracks. Vague prompts expand the solution space and force corrective passes. Precise prompts narrow it and converge faster. Over time, the difference shows up in total token consumption.My suspicion is that companies will eventually care less about raw vibe-coding speed and more about something like speed divided by tokens. Not explicitly at first, but implicitly through cost limits, internal tooling, and expectations."

Author: Karmaisabeachhh
Text: Cautionary - Using AI in your job instead of understanding code got my colleague fired I worked as a frontend engineer in a startup. The pay was great. There were a few backend engineers in my team because it is a backend-heavy application.  It is was an AI startup, so the CEO and upper management were bullish on AI. I personally think they were delusional, but I have 1YOE so I cannot really know or comment.  Thus, we were forced to use AI. A lot of it. I have had 1:1s which were entirely about how my “cursor usage was the lowest in the company” even though I have never missed a deadline. ONCE.  But this isn’t my story. A colleague of mine was a good engineer when he got out of college. Knew enough python and did a lot of DSA. He had also built a decent project. So he was under no circumstances underqualified.   The issue started when he joined the company (we joined at the same time). I had interned as a frontend engineer before, and I understood how important it was to estimate time to complete task, and how to estimate them. He didnt have internship experience, so my manager set timelines for him. Initially they were generous and he was writing code by hand. Eventually they got tighter and tighter, and at one point, he had to start using AI to meet deadlines.  I think this is where the problems started. I am unsure how to explain the problem exactly, but if I write a piece of code and it breaks a certain way, I can find out the exact line where it breaks to debug. In his case, he started understanding code in “chunks”. So, this function basically does this, even though we don’t know how.  I think another problem with AI code generation is, AI does not care if the file is fifteen thousand lines wrong. But after a point, it becomes a headache even looking at those files. So, this promotes fixing further bugs via. AI.  It was all well and good until, one day, we get a slack call at 11PM, saying something broke in production. We then had to spend the entire next day trying to find and fix the problem.  We found out that the problem was in his code. Digging through all that was no easy task. But apparently, he had generated some changes via cursor before, and the manager merged it by reviewing it with AI. The guy was promptly fired (this was his second bug in production).  I am sure there are responsible ways to use AI. I am sure we can all have our opinions on who is right and who is wrong. Just wanted to let yall know this happened.
